function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Compare FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA with FY2025 consolidated capex and Q1 2026 AI capex annualized. Which internal cash source appears most capable of supporting broader platform investment, and what risks arise if its growth slows?

steps.reasoning_content:

1. Find FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA
2. Find FY2025 consolidated capex
3. Find Q1 2026 AI capex (and annualize it)
4. Compare these figures
5. Analyze which internal cash source appears most capable of supporting broader platform investment
6. Discuss risks if growth slows

First, I need to parse the SpaceX S-1 filing to get this information.

| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Income (loss) from operations** | **$(657)** | **$4,423** | **$(6,355)** | **$(2,589)** |
| *Add:* | | | | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 757 | 2,376 | 3,568 | 6,701 |

| Impairment | 38 | — | — | 38 |

arguments.prompt: Find Connectivity segment revenue growth, subscriber numbers, and cash flow generation details for FY2025 in {{spacex_s1}}
Find AI segment capex breakdown, xAI infrastructure investment, and data center capital expenditures for Q1 2026 and FY2025 in {{spacex_s1}}
Find risk factors related to Connectivity segment growth slowing, Starlink subscriber growth, and capital expenditure requirements in {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here are the details regarding the Connectivity segment for FY2025:

### **1. Revenue Growth (FY2025)**
*   **Total Revenue:** Generated **$11,387 million**, representing a year-over-year growth of **49.8%** (an increase of $3,788 million compared to 2024).
*   **Revenue Drivers:** 
    *   *Consumer:* Increased by $2,377 million.
    *   *Enterprise & Government:* Increased by $1,411 million (driven by enterprise connectivity, mobile connectivity, and government business).
*   **Income from Operations:** **$4,423 million**, representing a massive year-over-year growth of **120.4%**.
*   **Segment Adjusted EBITDA:** **$7,168 million**, representing a year-over-year growth of **86.2%**.

### **2. Subscriber Numbers (FY2025)**
*   **Starlink Subscribers:** The text does not provide the absolute total number of Starlink subscribers, but it highlights a **99.9% growth in Starlink Subscribers** for the full year 2025. 
*   **ARPU Impact:** This subscriber growth was offset by an **11.2% decline in Starlink Subscriber ARPU** (Average Revenue Per User), primarily due to international expansion and the introduction of lower-priced service plans.
*   **Mobile Connectivity:** Through its mobile V1 Mobile satellite constellation, the company provided services to approximately **7.4 million monthly unique devices** across approximately 30 countries.

### **3. Cash Flow Generation Details (FY2025)**
*   **Not Available in Provided Text:** The provided excerpts **do not contain** specific cash flow generation details (such as Operating Cash Flow or Free Cash Flow) for the Connectivity segment in FY2025. 
*   *Note on Capital Expenditures:* The text begins to list 2025 capital expenditures across segments but cuts off abruptly due to a structural fragment break (*"In 2025, capital [... Structural Fragment Break ...]"*). 
*   *Proxy Metric:* The closest financial metric provided regarding cash-generating profitability is the **Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $7,168 million**. Additionally, the text notes a massive $19.6 billion equity and cash consideration agreement to acquire spectrum from EchoStar, though this is a capital commitment rather than a cash flow generation metric.

Based on the provided S-1 preamble and financial disclosures, here is the breakdown of the AI segment's capital expenditures, xAI infrastructure investments, and data center capital expenditures for Q1 2026 and FY 2025.

### 1. AI Segment CapEx Breakdown
The text outlines massive, accelerating capital expenditures for the AI segment to support compute and infrastructure scaling:
*   **Q1 2026 (Three Months Ended March 31, 2026):** **$7,723 million** (an increase of $5,156 million compared to Q1 2025).
*   **FY 2025 (Year Ended December 31, 2025):** **$12,727 million**.
*   *Historical Comparison:* Q1 2025 was $2,567 million; FY 2024 was $5,633 million; FY 2023 was $463 million.
*   **Primary Drivers:** The exponential increase in AI CapEx is primarily driven by the rapid expansion of terrestrial data centers, including the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities, supporting infrastructure, and GPU hardware deployments.

### 2. xAI Infrastructure Investment
*   **Acquisition & Segment Formation:** The company acquired xAI in **February 2026**, which now forms the foundation of its newly established AI segment.
*   **Investment Strategy & Horizon:** The company plans to allocate substantial capital to expand its compute infrastructure (including terrestrial and planned orbital data centers). Management explicitly warns of a **"multi-year investment horizon"** before these heavy deployments translate into sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA.
*   **Market Opportunity:** Capital expenditures are being scaled as quickly as power and compute can be deployed to capture a **$26.5 trillion** potential market opportunity for AI.
*   **Financing the Build-out:** To fund these infrastructure costs, the company is leveraging public market debt and equity. Notably, in FY 2025, proceeds from debt and financing arrangements specifically for the AI segment totaled **$16,055 million**.
*   **Financial Impact:** The heavy infrastructure build-out has resulted in significant operating losses (Q1 2026 AI loss from operations of $(2,469) million; FY 2025 loss of $(6,355) million) driven by cloud computing costs, data center operating expenses, and GPU hardware depreciation.

### 3. Data Center Capital Expenditures (Q1 2026 and FY 2025)
*Note: The provided text does not isolate "Data Center CapEx" as a standalone line item separate from the AI Segment or Total CapEx. However, the AI Segment CapEx serves as the direct proxy, as the MD&A explicitly attributes AI CapEx increases to data center build-outs.*

*   **Q1 2026 AI/Data Center CapEx:** **$7,723 million** (out of $10,107 million Total Company CapEx).
*   **FY 2025 AI/Data Center CapEx:** **$12,727 million** (out of $20,737 million Total Company CapEx).
*   **Capitalized Interest:** The company capitalizes interest during the construction period for significant long-term projects, specifically naming **"AI infrastructure data centers."** ($7 million was capitalized in Q1 2026 and added to Construction-in-progress).

**Related PP&E Asset Balances (Note 5):**
The scale of the data center investments is further reflected in the company's gross Property, Plant, and Equipment balances:
*   **Servers and networking equipment:** $23,850 million (Mar 31, 2026) / $22,694 million (Dec 31, 2025)
*   **Data center infrastructure:** $2,965 million (Mar 31, 2026) / $2,960 million (Dec 31, 2025)
*   **Construction-in-progress (CIP):** **$14,045 million** (Mar 31, 2026) / $4,604 million (Dec 31, 2025). The notes specify that CIP is primarily comprised of ongoing facility expansion and **AI infrastructure** that has not yet been placed in service.

Based on the provided S-1 and MD&A excerpts, here are the risk factors and operational challenges related to **Connectivity segment growth slowing**, **Starlink subscriber growth**, and **capital expenditure requirements**:

### 1. Risks Related to Connectivity Segment Growth Slowing
*   **Macroeconomic & Geopolitical Headwinds:** Economic downturns, inflation, higher interest rates, tighter credit conditions, and geopolitical developments can reduce consumer, enterprise, and government spending. This reduced demand could limit overall business growth and slow the pace at which the company deploys new satellites.
*   **Intense Market Competition:** The Connectivity segment faces heavy competition from terrestrial fixed network providers, mobile network operators, and other satellite providers. Starlink services may be less competitive in dense urban areas where terrestrial fiber and wireless networks offer higher capacity, lower costs, or more consistent performance.
*   **International Market Adoption:** Growth depends heavily on the ability to increase market awareness and acceptance of Starlink across numerous international markets, each of which presents unique regulatory, licensing, and operational challenges.
*   **Satellite-to-Mobile Partnership Dependencies:** Expanding satellite-to-mobile connectivity relies substantially on securing and maintaining partnerships with global telecommunications carriers and spectrum licensees, as well as the willingness of device manufacturers to adopt necessary hardware and software modifications.

### 2. Risks Related to Starlink Subscriber Growth
*   **Pricing & Distribution Constraints:** Efforts to acquire new subscribers may fail if the company cannot offer Starlink services at competitive prices. Additionally, constraints in the manufacturing or distribution of user terminals could delay service activations, increase costs, and limit the ability to scale.
*   **Consumer Perception & Terrestrial Alternatives:** Consumer acceptance may be hindered by the presence of well-established terrestrial broadband alternatives, as well as lingering perceptions regarding satellite internet's service reliability, latency, and setup complexity.
*   **Infrastructure Outages & Loss of Trust:** The complex interdependence of space and terrestrial infrastructure means that equipment malfunctions, cyberattacks, natural disasters, or geopolitical retaliation (e.g., from operating in conflict zones) could cause service outages. Such outages would erode trust and lead to the loss of existing and potential Starlink subscribers.
*   **ARPU Dilution (Monetization Headwinds):** While the subscriber base is growing rapidly (e.g., 104.7% YoY growth in Q1 2026), this is accompanied by significant declines in Average Revenue Per User (ARPU) (e.g., a 22.9% decline). This dilution is primarily driven by international expansion and the addition of lower-priced service plans, which pressures overall revenue growth despite high subscriber acquisition.

### 3. Risks Related to Capital Expenditure (CapEx) Requirements
*   **Massive Infrastructure & AI Funding Needs:** Developing and maintaining the technologies and infrastructure necessary for current and future operations requires immense capital expenditures. The newly acquired AI segment is particularly capital-intensive, requiring massive investments in compute, power generation, data centers, and model training (evidenced by $7.7 billion in AI CapEx in Q1 2026 alone).
*   **Cash Flow & Financing Vulnerabilities:** If the company is unable to generate sufficient cash flow from operations or secure additional financing on acceptable terms to fund these heavy CapEx demands, its financial condition, results of operations, and future prospects could be materially harmed.
*   **Fixed-Price Contract Overruns:** The company must absorb excess costs on fixed-price contracts if it experiences overruns due to inflation, supply chain disruptions, design changes, or technical challenges. This strains cash flows, reduces margins, and limits the ability to allocate capital to R&D, strategic initiatives, or future growth.
*   **Substantial Indebtedness & Material Commitments:** The company's substantial level of indebtedness could materially adversely affect its financial condition. This is compounded by massive material cash commitments, such as the pending $19.6 billion equity and cash acquisition of EchoStar spectrum licenses, which requires significant capital and regulatory approval.

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me also get the Connectivity segment capex for FY2025 and any additional details about the Space segment to complete the comparison.

**Space Segment Capital Expenditures:**

* **FY2024:** $2,032 million

Based on the provided text, here is the information regarding consolidated operating cash flow and free cash flow:

**Consolidated Operating Cash Flow**

**Free Cash Flow**

Furthermore, the total "Purchases of property, plant, and equipment" (Capital Expenditures) required to calculate Free Cash Flow are cut off or missing from the provided cash flow tables (only the related-party portions of those purchases are listed).

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the key data. Let me compile the analysis:

**Key Figures:**

1. **FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA**: $7,168 million
2. **FY2025 Consolidated Capex**: $20,737 million
   - Connectivity segment capex: $4,178 million
   - AI segment capex: $12,727 million
   - Space segment capex: $3,832 million
3. **Q1 2026 AI Capex**: $7,723 million → Annualized: $7,723 × 4 = $30,892 million

**Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA vs. its own capex:**
- Connectivity Adj. EBITDA: $7,168M
- Connectivity capex: $4,178M
- Connectivity "free cash flow proxy" (Adj. EBITDA - capex): $7,168 - $4,178 = $2,990M

**Comparison:**
- Connectivity Adj. EBITDA ($7,168M) covers only 34.6% of consolidated capex ($20,737M)
- Q1 2026 AI capex annualized ($30,892M) is 4.3x the Connectivity Adj. EBITDA
- Consolidated operating cash flow FY2025: $6,785M — even less than Connectivity Adj. EBITDA

**Which internal cash source is most capable?**
The Connectivity segment is clearly the most capable internal cash source. It generated $7,168M in Adjusted EBITDA against only $4,178M in capex, yielding a ~$2,990M surplus. The Space segment had only $653M in Adjusted EBITDA. The AI segment had negative Adjusted EBITDA of $(1,237)M.

**Risks if Connectivity growth slows:**
- ARPU dilution (11.2% decline in FY2025, 22.9% in Q1 2026)
- Subscriber growth may decelerate as markets saturate
- Competition from terrestrial providers
- If Connectivity EBITDA growth slows, the company would need to rely even more heavily on external financing (debt/equity) to fund the massive AI capex buildout
- The $19.6B EchoStar spectrum acquisition adds further capital pressure

Let me calculate a few more things for the analysis.
Let me also get the FY2025 AI segment capex vs FY2024 to show the growth trajectory, and any additional context about the Connectivity segment's margin profile.

Now I have all the data I need. Let me compile the comprehensive analysis.

Key data summary:
- FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $7,168M (62.9% margin on $11,387M revenue)
- FY2025 Connectivity capex: $4,178M
- Connectivity "surplus" (Adj. EBITDA - capex): $2,990M
- FY2025 Consolidated capex: $20,737M
- FY2025 AI segment capex: $12,727M
- FY2025 Space segment capex: $3,832M
- Q1 2026 AI capex: $7,723M → Annualized: $30,892M
- FY2025 Consolidated operating cash flow: $6,785M
- FY2025 Space segment Adj. EBITDA: $653M
- FY2025 AI segment Adj. EBITDA: $(1,237)M
- Total Reportable Segments Adj. EBITDA: $6,584M

The Connectivity segment is the only segment generating a positive surplus of Adjusted EBITDA over its own capex. It is the most capable internal cash source for supporting broader platform investment.

Risks if growth slows:
1. ARPU dilution is already significant (11.2% FY2025, 22.9% Q1 2026)
2. AI capex is accelerating rapidly (Q1 2026 annualized is 2.4x FY2025 AI capex)
3. Consolidated operating cash flow ($6,785M) is already deeply negative vs. consolidated capex ($20,737M), creating a $13,952M gap
4. The company relies heavily on external financing ($16,055M in AI-related debt/financing in FY2025)
5. EchoStar spectrum acquisition ($19.6B) adds further capital pressure
6. If Connectivity growth slows, the company would need to increase external financing or curtail AI investment

arguments.final_result:
## FY2025 Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA vs. Consolidated Capex vs. Q1 2026 AI Capex Annualized

### Key Figures (from the SpaceX S-1, recast consolidated basis)

| Metric | Amount |
|---|---|
| **FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$7,168M** |
| FY2025 Connectivity Segment Capex | $4,178M |
| **Connectivity Surplus (Adj. EBITDA − Capex)** | **$2,990M** |
| FY2025 Connectivity Adj. EBITDA Margin | 62.9% (on $11,387M revenue) |
| **FY2025 Consolidated Capex** | **$20,737M** |
| — AI Segment | $12,727M |
| — Connectivity Segment | $4,178M |
| — Space Segment | $3,832M |
| **Q1 2026 AI Segment Capex** | **$7,723M** |
| **Q1 2026 AI Capex Annualized (×4)** | **$30,892M** |
| FY2025 Consolidated Operating Cash Flow | $6,785M |

### Comparative Analysis

1. **Connectivity Adj. EBITDA covers only ~34.6% of FY2025 consolidated capex.** Even the Connectivity segment's robust $7.2 billion EBITDA is insufficient to fund the consolidated capital program on its own.

2. **Q1 2026 AI capex annualized ($30,892M) is 4.3× the entire FY2025 Connectivity Adj. EBITDA.** AI infrastructure spending accelerated from $12,727M in FY2025 to a $30,892M annualized run-rate in Q1 2026—a 143% step-up—driven by terrestrial data center build-outs and GPU deployments.

3. **Consolidated operating cash flow ($6,785M) fell $13,952M short of consolidated capex ($20,737M) in FY2025.** This gap was bridged by $16,055M in AI-segment debt and financing proceeds, underscoring the company's dependence on external capital markets.

4. **Segment-level cash generation is highly asymmetric:**
   - **Connectivity:** Adj. EBITDA $7,168M vs. capex $4,178M → **+$2,990M surplus**
   - **Space:** Adj. EBITDA $653M vs. capex $3,832M → **−$3,179M deficit**
   - **AI:** Adj. EBITDA $(1,237)M vs. capex $12,727M → **−$13,964M deficit**

### Which Internal Cash Source Is Most Capable?

**The Connectivity segment is unambiguously the most capable internal cash source for supporting broader platform investment.** It is the only segment generating a positive surplus of Adjusted EBITDA over its own capital needs, producing $2,990M in excess cash-proxy in FY2025. Key strengths include:

- **62.9% Adjusted EBITDA margin** on $11,387M in revenue, demonstrating exceptional operating leverage from the Starlink satellite constellation.
- **86.2% year-over-year Adj. EBITDA growth**, outpacing revenue growth (49.8%) as the segment scales.
- **99.9% subscriber growth** in FY2025, indicating the business is still in a high-growth phase.
- A relatively capital-light marginal growth model: each incremental subscriber requires minimal additional satellite infrastructure once the constellation is deployed.

No other segment comes close. The Space segment generated only $653M in Adj. EBITDA against $3,832M in capex, and the AI segment is deeply negative on both metrics.

### Risks If Connectivity Growth Slows

If Connectivity segment growth decelerates, several compounding risks emerge:

1. **ARPU Dilution Is Already Eroding Revenue Quality.** Starlink subscriber ARPU declined 11.2% in FY2025 and accelerated to a 22.9% decline in Q1 2026, driven by international expansion into lower-ARPU markets and the introduction of lower-priced service plans. Subscriber growth (104.7% in Q1 2026) must increasingly outrun ARPU headwinds just to sustain revenue momentum. If subscriber growth plateaus while ARPU continues to fall, EBITDA growth could stall.

2. **AI Capex Demands Are Accelerating Beyond Internal Funding Capacity.** At the Q1 2026 annualized run-rate of ~$30.9B, AI capex alone would consume 4.3× the Connectivity segment's entire FY2025 EBITDA. Any slowdown in Connectivity cash generation would widen the already-massive funding gap and force greater reliance on external debt and equity markets, which the company accessed for $16,055M in AI-related financing in FY2025 alone.

3. **EchoStar Spectrum Acquisition Adds $19.6B in Capital Commitments.** This pending acquisition, critical for mobile connectivity expansion, represents a substantial additional cash requirement that would compete with AI infrastructure for available internal and external capital.

4. **Competitive and Regulatory Saturation Risks.** Starlink faces competition from terrestrial fiber/5G in dense markets, regulatory and licensing hurdles across international jurisdictions, and dependency on telecom carrier partnerships for satellite-to-mobile services. A slowdown in market penetration would directly compress the EBITDA surplus that cross-subsidizes the broader platform.

5. **Consolidated Free Cash Flow Is Deeply Negative.** With consolidated operating cash flow of $6,785M against $20,737M in capex, the company generated negative free cash flow of approximately $(13,952)M in FY2025. If Connectivity—the sole internal cash engine—falters, the company's ability to service its substantial indebtedness and fund its multi-year AI investment horizon would be materially impaired.

6. **AI Segment Has a Multi-Year Path to Positive EBITDA.** Management explicitly warns of a "multi-year investment horizon" before AI investments generate sustained positive Adjusted EBITDA. During this period, Connectivity is effectively the only internal offset to AI cash burn. A Connectivity slowdown would leave external financing as the sole funding source, increasing leverage and refinancing risk.

### Conclusion

The Connectivity segment's $7,168M Adjusted EBITDA is the platform's critical internal cash engine, generating a $2,990M surplus after its own capex. However, this surplus covers only a fraction of the consolidated capital program—particularly as AI capex accelerates toward a ~$31B annualized run-rate. The company's investment thesis depends on Connectivity sustaining high growth while AI investments eventually reach scale. If Connectivity growth slows amid ARPU dilution and market saturation, the funding gap would widen materially, forcing increased dependence on capital markets at a time when the company is already taking on substantial debt.

---

**Sources:**

  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}

## FY2025 Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA vs. Consolidated Capex vs. Q1 2026 AI Capex Annualized

### Key Figures (from the SpaceX S-1, recast consolidated basis)

| Metric | Amount |
|---|---|
| **FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$7,168M** |
| FY2025 Connectivity Segment Capex | $4,178M |
| **Connectivity Surplus (Adj. EBITDA − Capex)** | **$2,990M** |
| FY2025 Connectivity Adj. EBITDA Margin | 62.9% (on $11,387M revenue) |
| **FY2025 Consolidated Capex** | **$20,737M** |
| — AI Segment | $12,727M |
| — Connectivity Segment | $4,178M |
| — Space Segment | $3,832M |
| **Q1 2026 AI Segment Capex** | **$7,723M** |
| **Q1 2026 AI Capex Annualized (×4)** | **$30,892M** |
| FY2025 Consolidated Operating Cash Flow | $6,785M |

### Comparative Analysis

1. **Connectivity Adj. EBITDA covers only ~34.6% of FY2025 consolidated capex.** Even the Connectivity segment's robust $7.2 billion EBITDA is insufficient to fund the consolidated capital program on its own.

3. **Consolidated operating cash flow ($6,785M) fell $13,952M short of consolidated capex ($20,737M) in FY2025.** This gap was bridged by $16,055M in AI-segment debt and financing proceeds, underscoring the company's dependence on external capital markets.

   - **Space:** Adj. EBITDA $653M vs. capex $3,832M → **−$3,179M deficit**
   - **AI:** Adj. EBITDA $(1,237)M vs. capex $12,727M → **−$13,964M deficit**

### Which Internal Cash Source Is Most Capable?

**The Connectivity segment is unambiguously the most capable internal cash source for supporting broader platform investment.** It is the only segment generating a positive surplus of Adjusted EBITDA over its own capital needs, producing $2,990M in excess cash-proxy in FY2025. Subscriber growth (104.7% in Q1 2026) must increasingly outrun ARPU headwinds just to sustain revenue momentum. If subscriber growth plateaus while ARPU continues to fall, EBITDA growth could stall.

2. **AI Capex Demands Are Accelerating Beyond Internal Funding Capacity.** At the Q1 2026 annualized run-rate of ~$30.9B, AI capex alone would consume 4.3× the Connectivity segment's entire FY2025 EBITDA. Any slowdown in Connectivity cash generation would widen the already-massive funding gap and force greater reliance on external debt and equity markets, which the company accessed for $16,055M in AI-related financing in FY2025 alone.

3. **EchoStar Spectrum Acquisition Adds $19.6B in Capital Commitments.** This pending acquisition, critical for mobile connectivity expansion, represents a substantial additional cash requirement that would compete with AI infrastructure for available internal and external capital.

4. **Competitive and Regulatory Saturation Risks.** Starlink faces competition from terrestrial fiber/5G in dense markets, regulatory and licensing hurdles across international jurisdictions, and dependency on telecom carrier partnerships for satellite-to-mobile services. A slowdown in market penetration would directly compress the EBITDA surplus that cross-subsidizes the broader platform.

5. **Consolidated Free Cash Flow Is Deeply Negative.** With consolidated operating cash flow of $6,785M against $20,737M in capex, the company generated negative free cash flow of approximately $(13,952)M in FY2025. If Connectivity—the sole internal cash engine—falters, the company's ability to service its substantial indebtedness and fund its multi-year AI investment horizon would be materially impaired.

6. **AI Segment Has a Multi-Year Path to Positive EBITDA.** Management explicitly warns of a "multi-year investment horizon" before AI investments generate sustained positive Adjusted EBITDA. During this period, Connectivity is effectively the only internal offset to AI cash burn. A Connectivity slowdown would leave external financing as the sole funding source, increasing leverage and refinancing risk.

### Conclusion

However, this surplus covers only a fraction of the consolidated capital program—particularly as AI capex accelerates toward a ~$31B annualized run-rate. The company's investment thesis depends on Connectivity sustaining high growth while AI investments eventually reach scale.
